summary:
The Dunkirk evacuation, codenamed Operation Dynamo, took place from May 26 to June 4, 1940, when Allied forces trapped on the beaches and harbor of Dunkirk in northern France were evacuated to England. The operation followed Germany's rapid conquest of the Low Countries and the encirclement of Allied forces by German armored units. Approximately 338,000 British, French, and Belgian troops were rescued across the English Channel, an outcome made possible partly by the controversial German decision to halt the armored advance for several days — a decision whose precise reasons remain debated by historians.

The evacuation was conducted by a fleet of Royal Navy vessels supplemented by hundreds of civilian boats — fishing vessels, ferries, yachts, and pleasure craft — that crossed the Channel in what became celebrated in British memory as the spirit of Dunkirk. The operation rescued the core of the British Army that would later fight the war, though enormous quantities of equipment and weapons were abandoned on the beaches. Nine naval destroyers were sunk and many other vessels lost.

Churchill's speech to the House of Commons following Dunkirk was one of the war's defining moments of oratory. He acknowledged the reality of a rescue while denying it was a victory, and called on Britain to fight on. The Dunkirk evacuation became mythologized in British culture as a story of resilience and improvisation against desperate odds, though historians have also emphasized that it was fundamentally a military defeat that left Britain momentarily unable to continue land operations against Germany.
